source.title: US Ink/Sun Chemical Corporation Ink Dust Explosion and Flash Fires in East Rutherford, New Jersey

event.top_event: Combustible dust explosion and flash fires in dust collection system ductwork
event.incident_type: Dust explosion with secondary flash fires
actions_taken: Fire sprinkler system activated and extinguished fires
Firefighters detected and extinguished ductwork fires
Black ink production halted until end of December 2012
event.summary: On October 9, 2012, at approximately 1:15 pm EST, a flash fire caused burn injuries to seven workers at the US Ink manufacturing facility in East Rutherford, New Jersey. The incident began with a flash fire at the bag dump station, followed by a loud thumping sound from the rooftop dust collection system. Workers congregated at the pre-mix room doorway to investigate. A large flash fire then erupted from above mixing tank T-306, engulfing seven employees in flames. Three employees sustained third-degree burns. The root cause was spontaneous self-ignition of accumulated sludge-like material and combustible dust mixture (Gilsonite, carbon black, and clay) in the ductwork of a newly installed dust collection system. The system had been commissioned only four days prior and continued operating continuously despite a design malfunction, allowing condensable vapors to accumulate and self-heat in the ducts.
recommendations: Conduct comprehensive hazard assessments for combustible dust hazards
Implement automatic temperature control systems for mixing tanks
Design dust collection systems to handle condensable vapors
Provide flame-resistant clothing (FRC) to all workers in areas with flash fire or explosion hazards
Implement audible and visual alarms for explosion suppression system activation
Establish stop-work authority for employees to shut down faulty equipment
Conduct thorough system performance testing and commissioning before operation
Implement management of change procedures for new equipment installation
Provide comprehensive training on new equipment systems
Establish preventive maintenance and inspection protocols for dust collection systems

hazards.name: Lack of temperature control in mixing process

threats.name: Self-heating and spontaneous ignition of duct sludge
Electrostatic discharge ignition of Gilsonite dust
Dust collection system malfunction allowing continuous operation
Inadequate commissioning and system performance testing
Removal of wet scrubber system without adequate replacement design

consequences.name: Flash fire in dust collection ductwork
Pressure rise and dust explosion in collector
Flame propagation back through ductwork to pre-mix room
Large flash fire engulfing workers at pre-mix room entrance
Thermal injuries to seven employees (three third-degree burns)

controls.name: Dust collection system design and installation
controls.barrier_role: Prevent accumulation of combustible dust in pre-mix room
controls.lod_basis: Primary engineering control to manage particulate emissions

supporting_text: The new dust collection system was installed to improve the management of particulate material and produce an overall improvement in the operating conditions of the black ink production process.
The dust collection system consisted of a branching system of various sizes of ducts, including flexible connectors attached to the top of each mixing tank and to the bag dump station.
The flash fire occurred in the dust collection system during the first day of normal production after initial equipment start-up, on Tuesday, October 9, 2012.

controls.name: Automatic start/stop control of dust collection system
controls.barrier_role: Prevent continuous operation and vapor accumulation
controls.lod_basis: Designed to synchronize dust collection with mixing tank operation

supporting_text: However, the dust collection system actually continued to run overnight, even when all the ink mixers were shut off.

A US Ink maintenance employee reported in an interview that upon his arrival on Saturday, although the mixing tanks were shut down the night before, the dust collection system had run all night, a departure from the initial design intent of automatic start-up and shutdown, in sync with the mixing tanks.

Although the maintenance employee reported the dust collection system equipment malfunction to his superiors and to the electrical contractor that wired the dust collection system, US Ink/Sun Chemical Corporation management took no action to immediately investigate the failure or to shut down the ink mixing operation until the malfunction was corrected.

controls.name: Fike explosion suppression and isolation system
controls.barrier_role: Suppress dust explosion and prevent flame propagation
controls.lod_basis: Designed to respond to rapid pressure rise in dust collector

supporting_text: The dust collector included an explosion suppression system (shown in Figure 4 and Figure 5), which would actuate and release the suppressant, sodium bicarbonate, if a rapid pressure increase occurred in the dust collector.

The design intent focused on preventing damaging explosive overpressures in the collector and inlet duct in the event of a drastic pressure rise and on containing any explosion hazard within the dust collector.

Although the ignition led to a dust explosion within the dust collector, the pressure rise activated the Fike explosion suppression system, which prevented the structural failure of the dust collector.
The Fike explosion suppression and isolation system prevented the structural failure of the dust collector by suppressing the deflagration and isolating the dust collector as designed.

Another employee who approached the pre-mix room area noticed that the lights on the alarm panel for the dust collector explosion suppression system were red, indicating detection of a pressure rise and activation of the system. However, this system did not produce an audible alarm.

controls.name: Fire sprinkler system in pre-mix room
controls.barrier_role: Extinguish fires and limit fire propagation
controls.lod_basis: Automatic fire suppression system for enclosed space

supporting_text: An automatic sprinkler system was installed as a fire protection feature in the pre-mix room.

The sprinkler system was also connected to an automatic audible alarm. Once the sprinkler system was activated, an automatic signal was relayed via an external central monitoring station to the local fire department for immediate response.
The flash fire triggered the fire sprinkler system in the pre-mix room.

After they arrived and entered the plant, East Rutherford Fire Department personnel did not see any flames in the pre-mix room because the sprinklers had extinguished fires outside of the enclosed equipment.

controls.name: Process hazard analysis and management of change
controls.barrier_role: Identify hazards and control risks during equipment changes
controls.lod_basis: Management system to assess and control process modifications

supporting_text: KEY ISSUES: Combustible Dust, Engineering Design, Management of Change, Process Hazard Analysis, Hazard Communication, Management Oversight, Regulatory Oversight

The CSB concluded that the explosion and flash fires occurred because of continuous manually controlled heating of the mixing tanks and operation of the dust collection system for several hours after commissioning, with the system continuing to draw condensable vapors into the duct.

After installation of the dust collection system, operators in the pre-mix room could not observe the vapor and use it as an indicator because the dust collection system metal ducts were connected to the opening of the mixing tank heads. In addition, after removal of the scrubber system, the combustible vapor generated by the ink mixing operation could no longer be eliminated because the new dust collection system was not designed to release condensable vapor.

controls.name: System commissioning and performance testing
controls.barrier_role: Verify system design and operation before production use
controls.lod_basis: Quality assurance process for new equipment installation

supporting_text: The new dust collection system was commissioned at the facility in the week preceding the incident.

At commissioning, US Ink employees who would operate the system (several black ink production supervisors and one of the day-shift operators) received 15 minutes of operational training and instruction as well as a walkthrough of the Fike explosion suppression and isolation system.

The engineer retired before the dust collection system was installed and commissioned on Friday, October 5, 2012. In Section 6, Safety Management Analysis, of this report, the CSB concludes that the engineer's retirement led to a less comprehensive design review and commissioning process for the dust collection system.
Best practices indicate that US Ink should not have accepted the new dust collector as complete if system start-up checks revealed that the audible alarms were not working as expected.

controls.name: Automatic temperature control for mixing tanks
controls.barrier_role: Prevent excessive temperatures that enhance vapor generation
controls.lod_basis: Process control to maintain safe operating temperatures

supporting_text: With no automatic temperature control and no guidelines, plant operators relied on individual instincts, observations of vapor from combustible liquids, and sounds generated during the mixing process as the basis for manually adjusting (from the control room) the speed of the tank mixing agitators.
Despite the presence of temperature gauges and recorders, no temperature control system governed the mixing tanks and that the ink mixing process design did not define any safe temperatures.

The heat generated as the solids are dissolved in the mixing tank increases the solution temperature to about 240°F. This increase in operating temperature is useful because it ensures complete dispersion of the solid materials into the liquid phase; it also contributes to greater evolution of condensable vapors.

controls.name: Flame-resistant clothing (FRC) for workers
controls.barrier_role: Reduce severity of thermal injuries from flash fire
controls.lod_basis: Personal protective equipment for flash fire hazard

supporting_text: The employees were not wearing flame-resistant clothing (FRC).

To reduce the risk of thermal injury from flash fire incidents in production-related operations, workers are required to wear FRC. As part of its personal protective equipment (PPE) standard (29 CFR 1910.132), OSHA requires employers to provide workers with FRC in workplaces when flash fire or explosion hazards are present.

Because the US Ink facility did generate dusts, the potential existed for a dust explosion, flash fire, or both. If US Ink had followed OSHA standards on hazard assessment and PPE selection, the US Ink/Sun Chemical safety managers would have identified 'harmful dust' and provided additional PPE.
The CSB learned that no corporate policy required the use of FRC by US Ink plant employees.

controls.name: Hazard communication and worker training
controls.barrier_role: Ensure workers understand combustible dust hazards
controls.lod_basis: Information and training on process hazards

supporting_text: At commissioning, US Ink employees who would operate the system (several black ink production supervisors and one of the day-shift operators) received 15 minutes of operational training and instruction as well as a walkthrough of the Fike explosion suppression and isolation system.

US Ink/Sun Chemical claimed that each operator was trained to monitor the mixer temperature to ensure that it remained sufficiently low and to adjust the mixing operation accordingly to mitigate high mixing temperatures.
KEY ISSUES: Hazard Communication

controls.name: Management oversight and stop-work authority
controls.lod_basis: Management system to enforce safety protocols

supporting_text: US Ink/Sun Chemical Corporation management did not give stop-work authority to the maintenance employee or the night-shift pre-mix room operator to shut down dust collection system operation once a malfunction was reported.

Although the maintenance employee reported the dust collection system equipment malfunction to his superiors and to the electrical contractor that wired the dust collection system, US Ink/Sun Chemical Corporation management took no action to immediately investigate the failure or to shut down the ink mixing operation until the malfunction was corrected.
This lapse in management oversight allowed the dust collection system to continue running during the ink mixing operation until the system eventually failed on Tuesday, October 9, 2012.

controls.name: Audible alarm for explosion suppression system activation
controls.barrier_role: Alert workers to explosion suppression system activation

supporting_text: However, this system did not produce an audible alarm.

US Ink/Sun Chemical maintained that Fike did not fulfill its requirement to fully install and ensure the operation of an audible alarm, despite providing an initial specification that indicated the presence of an audible alarm.

The local audible output might not have been heard by US Ink employees on the day of the incident due to the room ambient noise in the room. Also, US Ink did not incorporate any other area or building-wide audible or visual alarm or emergency responder notifications into their notification strategy.

controls.name: Design consideration of condensable vapors in dust collection system
controls.barrier_role: Prevent accumulation of flammable vapors in ductwork
controls.lod_basis: Engineering design to manage process emissions

supporting_text: After installation of the dust collection system, operators in the pre-mix room could not observe the vapor and use it as an indicator because the dust collection system metal ducts were connected to the opening of the mixing tank heads. In addition, after removal of the scrubber system, the combustible vapor generated by the ink mixing operation could no longer be eliminated because the new dust collection system was not designed to release condensable vapor. The vapors subsequently were trapped within the ductwork of the dust collection system.
Section 5, Engineering Design Analysis, discusses the lack of consideration of the presence of condensable vapor in the ductwork.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.

pif_evidence.people_competence: Operators received only 15 minutes of training on the new dust collection system. One operator relied on individual instincts and observations rather than systematic procedures to adjust mixing speeds.
pif_evidence.people_communication: Maintenance employee reported equipment malfunction to superiors but management took no action. Workers heard loud thumping but did not understand it as a warning sign of danger.
pif_evidence.people_situational_awareness: Workers congregated at the pre-mix room entrance after hearing the initial flash and thumping sound, placing themselves directly in the path of the subsequent large flash fire. They did not recognize the danger signals.
pif_evidence.work_procedures: No automatic temperature control system existed; operators manually adjusted mixing speeds based on instinct and vapor observation. After dust collection system installation, operators could no longer observe vapor as an indicator. No defined safe operating temperatures.
pif_evidence.work_tools_equipment: Dust collection system had design malfunction (continuous operation when mixers were off) that was not corrected before production resumed. Audible alarm on explosion suppression system was not functional or audible.
pif_evidence.organisation_safety_culture: Management did not give stop-work authority to employees to shut down faulty equipment. No corporate policy required flame-resistant clothing despite combustible dust hazards. No comprehensive hazard assessment was conducted.
pif_evidence.organisation_management_of_change: Replacement of wet scrubber system with new dust collection system was not properly evaluated for hazards. New system design did not account for condensable vapors. Lead engineer retired before commissioning, reducing design review rigor.
pif_evidence.organisation_supervision: Management took no action when maintenance employee reported dust collection system malfunction. Supervisors did not ensure equipment was properly tested before production resumed.
pif_evidence.organisation_training: Only 15 minutes of operational training provided at commissioning. No comprehensive training on combustible dust hazards. No training on recognizing warning signs of equipment malfunction.
